title: Homotopically Non-Trivial Maps with Small k-Dilation
overall_summary: The paper studies when bounds on k-dimensional volume expansion force a map between spheres to be homotopically trivial, and shows that for k at least 3 the answer can be very different from the 2-dilation case. Its core construction embeds a highly anisotropic rectangle in a sphere, maps one factor with large Lipschitz constant and another factor with very small Lipschitz constant, and uses a singular-value count to make every k-dimensional Jacobian small while preserving a suspended homotopy class. The paper also surveys lower-bound mechanisms for k-dilation, especially Tsui-Wang and Gromov results for small 2-dilation and Gromov's Hopf-invariant estimate via differential forms and Hodge theory. For the original rectangle problem, the most useful extracted content is the anisotropic product-map estimate, the exterior-power singular-value bookkeeping, and the contrast between rank-collapse constructions and degree or cohomological lower bounds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Basic singular-value estimates for k-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a C^1 map f between Riemannian manifolds, the k-dilation is the supremum over the domain of the operator norm |Λ^k df|. If the domain dimension is m and the singular values of df at a point are ordered as 0 ≤ s_1 ≤ ... ≤ s_m, then |Λ^k df| at that point is s_{m-k+1}...s_m, the product of the k largest singular values. Consequently, for 1 ≤ k < m, one has the pointwise inequality |Λ^{k+1} df| ≤ |Λ^k df|^{(k+1)/k}; hence if f has k-dilation D, then f has (k+1)-dilation at most D^{(k+1)/k}. Also, if F is L-Lipschitz, then the k-dilation of F ∘ f is at most L^k times the k-dilation of f.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: At a point, choose orthonormal bases diagonalizing df in singular-value form. On a decomposable basis k-vector e_{i_1} ∧ ... ∧ e_{i_k}, the exterior derivative Λ^k df multiplies lengths by s_{i_1}...s_{i_k}; the operator norm is therefore the largest such product, namely the product of the k largest singular values. If P = s_{m-k+1}...s_m, then s_{m-k} ≤ P^{1/k}, because each of the k factors in P is at least s_{m-k}. Thus |Λ^{k+1} df| = s_{m-k}P ≤ P^{(k+1)/k}. The composition estimate follows from Λ^k d(F ∘ f) = Λ^k dF ∘ Λ^k df and |Λ^k dF| ≤ L^k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1, suspension classes with arbitrarily small k-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a homotopy class a in π_m(S^n). Let Σ^p a denote its p-fold suspension, an element of π_{m+p}(S^{n+p}). If k is an integer satisfying k > n + (n/m)p, then the suspended class Σ^p a can be represented by maps S^{m+p} → S^{n+p} with arbitrarily small k-dilation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Represent a by a C^1 map f_1: [0,1]^m → S^n that sends the boundary of the cube to the basepoint. Choose a C^1 degree-one map f_2: [0,1]^p → S^p that sends the boundary to the basepoint. Let L be larger than the Lipschitz constants of both f_1 and f_2. For small ε > 0, the paper uses a quasi-isometric embedding, with ε-independent quasi-isometry constant, of the rectangle R_ε = [0,ε]^m × [0,ε^{-m/p}]^p into the unit sphere S^{m+p}. Define F = F_1 × F_2: R_ε → S^n × S^p by rescaling the first factor [0,ε]^m to [0,1]^m and applying f_1, and rescaling the second factor [0,ε^{-m/p}]^p to [0,1]^p and applying f_2. Thus F_1 has Lipschitz constant at most Lε^{-1}, while F_2 has Lipschitz constant at most Lε^{m/p}. Since the first target factor has dimension n, at most n singular directions can contribute the large factor Lε^{-1}; when k > n, any k-dimensional Jacobian must include at least k-n directions from the second, shrunken factor. The paper obtains the estimate Dil_k(F) < (Lε^{-1})^n(Lε^{m/p})^{k-n} = L^k ε^{-n + (m/p)k - (m/p)n} = L^k ε^{(m/p)(k - n - (n/m)p)}. The assumed inequality makes the exponent of ε positive, so the k-dilation tends to zero as ε → 0. The boundary of R_ε maps into S^n ∨ S^p. Composing with a degree-one smash map S^n × S^p → S^{n+p} that sends S^n ∨ S^p to the basepoint, and extending by the basepoint outside R_ε in S^{m+p}, gives a map S^{m+p} → S^{n+p}. The resulting map represents the p-fold suspension Σ^p a, and the fixed quasi-isometry and smash-map constants do not prevent the k-dilation from tending to zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example after Proposition 1, nontrivial S^4 to S^3 maps with arbitrarily small 3-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The nontrivial element of π_4(S^3), represented by the suspension of the Hopf fibration, can be represented by maps S^4 → S^3 with arbitrarily small 3-dilation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Proposition 1 to the Hopf fibration class in π_3(S^2) with p = 1, m = 3, n = 2, and k = 3. The numerical condition is 3 > 2 + (2/3)·1, which holds. The proposition therefore produces maps in the suspended Hopf class, i.e. the nontrivial element of π_4(S^3), whose 3-dilation tends to zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1, infinitely many nontrivial sphere maps with arbitrarily small 3-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every integer N ≥ 2, there are infinitely many integers M such that there exist homotopically nontrivial maps S^M → S^N with arbitrarily small 3-dilation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The topological input used in the paper is as follows. For i = 8j + 1, π_i(SO) is Z_2. Adams proved that the stable J-homomorphism is injective on these groups, producing nontrivial stable homotopy classes. The paper uses results of Curtis, and the formulation in Davis-Mahowald, to choose for each such i a class a_i in π_{i+2}(S^2) whose p-fold suspension is nontrivial for every p. Fix N ≥ 2 and choose i = 8j + 1 with i > 2N - 6. Consider the class Σ^{N-2}a_i in π_{i+N}(S^N), and put M = i + N. This class is nontrivial by the topological input. Apply Proposition 1 with p = N - 2, m = i + 2, n = 2, and k = 3. The condition in Proposition 1 becomes 3 > 2 + (2/(i+2))(N-2), which is exactly i > 2N - 6. Therefore each such nontrivial class has representatives with arbitrarily small 3-dilation, and there are infinitely many allowable i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Cited theorem of Tsui and Wang, small 2-dilation implies nullhomotopy between spheres
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f: S^m → S^n be a C^1 map between round spheres, with m ≥ 2. If the 2-dilation of f is less than 1, then f is nullhomotopic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove the theorem, but summarizes the method of Tsui and Wang. They consider the graph of f as a submanifold of S^m × S^n and evolve it by mean curvature flow. Under the hypothesis that the 2-dilation is less than 1, the flow remains graphical, so at each time it is the graph of a map f_t. The flow converges to the graph of a constant map, and the maps f_t provide a homotopy from f to a constant map.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Cited Gromov small-2-dilation nullhomotopy estimates
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper recalls Gromov's earlier lower-bound results for 2-dilation. First, for each pair of dimensions m and n, there is ε(m,n) > 0 such that every C^1 map S^m → S^n with 2-dilation less than ε(m,n) is nullhomotopic. Second, Gromov proves stronger variants: if |Λ^2 df| has sufficiently small L^{m-1+δ} norm, with threshold ε(m,n,δ), then f: S^m → S^n is nullhomotopic; and for any compact simply connected Riemannian manifolds M and N, there is ε(M,N) > 0 such that any map M → N with 2-dilation less than ε(M,N) is nullhomotopic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper only sketches Gromov's argument for the sphere case. Gromov treats a map S^m → S^n as a family of maps S^2 → S^n. If the 2-dilation is less than ε, each image S^2 has area less than 4πε. By the Riemann mapping theorem, one changes coordinates on the domain spheres so that each map in the family has Dirichlet energy bounded by Cε. The borderline Sobolev inequality then bounds the BMO norm of each map by Cε. From the BMO bound, Gromov constructs a homotopy from the original family to a family of constant maps. The paper cites these as special cases of Gromov's corollary on page 230 of reference [5].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Elementary degree-volume lower bound for n-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If f: S^n → S^n is a C^1 map that is homotopically nontrivial, then its n-dilation is at least 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If the n-dilation were at most 1 - ε for some ε > 0, then the image of S^n under f would have n-dimensional volume at most (1 - ε)Vol(S^n). Hence f could not be surjective. A non-surjective map into S^n has image contained in S^n minus a point, which is contractible, so f would be nullhomotopic. This contradicts homotopical nontriviality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Gromov Hopf-invariant lower bound for 2n-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f: S^{4n-1} → S^{2n} be a sufficiently smooth map with 2n-dilation D. Then the absolute value of the Hopf invariant of f is bounded above by C(n)D^2, where C(n) depends only on n. Since the Hopf invariant is an integer, any map with nonzero Hopf invariant has 2n-dilation at least C(n)^{-1/2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a 2n-form ω on S^{2n} with ∫_{S^{2n}}ω = 1 and pointwise norm bounded by a constant C depending on n. The pullback f^*ω is a closed 2n-form on S^{4n-1}; because H^{2n}(S^{4n-1}) = 0, it is exact. Let P f^*ω be a primitive, so d(P f^*ω) = f^*ω. The Hopf invariant is ∫_{S^{4n-1}} P f^*ω ∧ f^*ω. The pointwise norm |f^*ω| is bounded by C times |Λ^{2n}df|, hence by CD, so the L^2 norm of f^*ω is at most CD after absorbing the fixed volume of the sphere into C. By Hodge theory, choose the primitive P f^*ω perpendicular to all exact (2n-1)-forms. For this choice, a Poincare-type estimate gives ||P f^*ω||_{L^2} ≤ λ^{-1/2}CD, where λ > 0 is the smallest eigenvalue of the Laplacian on exact 2n-forms on S^{4n-1}. Finally, Cauchy-Schwarz bounds |∫ P f^*ω ∧ f^*ω| by the product of the two L^2 norms, giving |Hopf(f)| ≤ C(n)D^2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Formal properties of the filtration V_kπ_m
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finite simplicial complex W equipped with the piecewise Riemannian metric in which each simplex is equilateral of edge length 1, define V_kπ_m(W) to be the set of homotopy classes in π_m(W) that can be represented by maps S^m → W with k-dilation tending to zero. The paper proves that V_kπ_m(W) is a subgroup of π_m(W), that V_kπ_m(W) ⊂ V_{k+1}π_m(W), and records the filtration 0 = V_1π_m(W) ⊂ V_2π_m(W) ⊂ ... ⊂ V_mπ_m(W) ⊂ V_{m+1}π_m(W) = π_m(W). It also proves naturality: if F: W → V is a continuous pointed map between finite simplicial complexes, then F_* maps V_kπ_m(W) into V_kπ_m(V). For an arbitrary space X, the paper defines a class a in π_m(X) to lie in V_kπ_m(X) if a is the image of an element of V_kπ_m(W) for some finite simplicial complex W and some map W → X; with this definition the filtration is functorial and homotopy invariant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For additive inverses, if f_i represents a class a with k-dilation tending to zero and I: S^m → S^m is a reflection of degree -1 fixing the basepoint, then f_i ∘ I represents -a and still has k-dilation tending to zero up to a fixed multiplicative constant. For sums, if f_i represents a and g_i represents b with k-dilations tending to zero, take a fixed map I: S^m → S^m ∨ S^m of degree (1,1). Define h_i on the wedge by f_i on the first sphere and g_i on the second. Then h_i ∘ I represents a + b, and its k-dilation tends to zero because I is fixed and h_i has k-dilation controlled by the maximum of the two pieces. The inclusion V_k ⊂ V_{k+1} follows from the singular-value inequality |Λ^{k+1}df| ≤ |Λ^kdf|^{(k+1)/k}. Also, any map from S^m has zero (m+1)-dilation, giving V_{m+1} = π_m. For naturality, approximate F by a PL map with finite Lipschitz constant L; then Dil_k(F ∘ f_i) ≤ L^k Dil_k(f_i), so k-dilation tending to zero is preserved under F_*. The definition for arbitrary spaces is then arranged so that the same naturality statement is automatic, and homotopy invariance follows from functoriality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2, computation of V_4π_7(S^4)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The group V_4π_7(S^4) is exactly the torsion subgroup of π_7(S^4). Since π_7(S^4) is isomorphic to Z ⊕ Z_12, this says V_4π_7(S^4) is the Z_12 summand; in particular it is a proper nonzero subgroup.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let H: π_7(S^4) → Z be the Hopf invariant. The Gromov Hopf-invariant estimate with n = 2 says that any map S^7 → S^4 with nonzero Hopf invariant has 4-dilation bounded below by a positive constant. Therefore any class representable with arbitrarily small 4-dilation must lie in ker H, so V_4π_7(S^4) ⊂ ker H. The paper recalls that ker H is isomorphic to Z_12 and that the suspension map π_6(S^3) → π_7(S^4) is an isomorphism onto ker H, as follows from the long exact sequence of the Hopf fibration S^3 → S^7 → S^4. Hence every element of ker H is a suspension. Applying Proposition 1 with p = 1, m = 6, n = 3, and k = 4 gives arbitrarily small 4-dilation for every such suspension class, because 4 > 3 + (3/6). Thus ker H ⊂ V_4π_7(S^4), proving equality.

proof_techniques.name: Anisotropic rectangular product construction
proof_techniques.description: The paper's main construction maps a rectangular block R_ε = [0,ε]^m × [0,ε^{-m/p}]^p into S^n × S^p. On the short m-dimensional factor, the map is a rescaled representative of a homotopy class and has Lipschitz scale ε^{-1}; on the long p-dimensional factor, it is a rescaled degree-one map to S^p and has Lipschitz scale ε^{m/p}. Because the first target has only n dimensions, a k-dimensional Jacobian with k > n must include some of the shrunken directions from the second factor. The resulting estimate is a competition between n large singular values and k-n small singular values.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is directly relevant to rectangle problems because it shows how anisotropic side lengths and rank limitations can make k-dimensional volume expansion small even when some directions are greatly stretched. For a degree-one 4-rectangle map with 2-dilation control, an analogous singular-value analysis can help identify which rank-collapse patterns must be ruled out by the boundary and degree hypotheses.

proof_techniques.name: Exterior-power singular-value bookkeeping
proof_techniques.description: A recurring estimate in the paper is that |Λ^k df| is the product of the k largest singular values of df. This allows the author to estimate k-dilation by counting how many large singular values can occur and how many small singular values must appear in every k-fold product. The same calculation also yields monotonicity of the small-dilation condition: if k-dilation tends to zero, then all higher dilations tend to zero as well.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For maps between 4-dimensional rectangles, Dil_2(f) ≤ 1 means the product of the two largest singular values is bounded by 1 almost everywhere. This singular-value viewpoint is a natural local input for deriving global constraints involving products of side lengths such as R_3R_4 or total volume.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary collapse and smash product to encode relative topology
proof_techniques.description: In Proposition 1, maps from cubes that send their boundary to a basepoint are used as representatives of maps from spheres. The product map sends the boundary of the product rectangle to the wedge S^n ∨ S^p, and a smash map S^n × S^p → S^{n+p} collapses the wedge to the basepoint. Extending by the basepoint outside the embedded rectangle gives a sphere map in the desired suspended homotopy class.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem also concerns maps of pairs (R,∂R) → (S,∂S), so collapsing boundaries to obtain sphere-level or quotient-level topology is a natural maneuver. The paper's construction indicates how relative boundary conditions can preserve nontrivial topology while the differential is highly anisotropic.

proof_techniques.name: Differential-form lower bounds for homotopy invariants
proof_techniques.description: For the Hopf invariant, the paper presents Gromov's method: pull back a normalized volume form, choose a controlled primitive using Hodge theory, and estimate the defining integral for the invariant by L^2 norms. The k-dilation enters because the pullback of a k-form is bounded pointwise by the k-dilation times the form norm.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A degree-one rectangle map has a cohomological or integral invariant analogous in spirit to the Hopf invariant, although simpler. This suggests trying to pair pulled-back coordinate or volume forms with chains or slices and use Dil_2 bounds plus geometry of R and S to force quantitative lower bounds.

proof_techniques.name: Small 2-dilation as a topological obstruction
proof_techniques.description: The paper recalls two types of 2-dilation lower bounds: Tsui-Wang's sharp theorem that 2-dilation less than 1 for maps S^m → S^n implies nullhomotopy, and Gromov's earlier theorem that sufficiently small 2-dilation, or even sufficiently small L^{m-1+δ} norm of |Λ^2df|, implies nullhomotopy. These results are topological obstructions specifically tied to two-dimensional area contraction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem assumes a 2-dilation bound and a nonzero degree condition. One possible strategy is to normalize, double, or quotient the rectangle map into a sphere or compact-manifold map where a small-2-dilation nullhomotopy theorem would contradict the degree.

proof_techniques.name: Functorial control of k-dilation under Lipschitz postcomposition
proof_techniques.description: The filtration argument uses the simple but important estimate that postcomposition by an L-Lipschitz map multiplies k-dilation by at most L^k. This lets the paper pass from maps into a finite complex to maps into another complex, and shows that the property of having representatives with arbitrarily small k-dilation is stable under fixed Lipschitz operations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In rectangle comparisons one often rescales source and target rectangles to normalized boxes or composes with coordinate projections and inclusions. This estimate tracks how the Dil_2 bound changes under such normalizations and fixed geometric maps.

proof_techniques.name: Hodge-theoretic primitive estimate
proof_techniques.description: In the Hopf-invariant argument, after writing f^*ω as an exact form, the primitive is not chosen arbitrarily. It is chosen perpendicular to exact forms so that a spectral gap for the Hodge Laplacian gives an L^2 bound on the primitive in terms of the L^2 norm of f^*ω. This converts pointwise k-dilation control into a bound on a global integral invariant.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the rectangle problem is attacked through forms on a doubled or quotient manifold, a similar primitive or Poincare-type estimate may help convert area-dilation bounds into inequalities involving side lengths and volumes.

other_useful_info: The paper points to Gromov's Metric Structures for Riemannian and Non-Riemannian Spaces, pages 358-359, for the Hopf-invariant estimate, and to Gromov's Carnot-Caratheodory spaces as seen from within, especially pages 179 and 220-230, for small-2-dilation and related lower bounds. Section 4 gives additional examples of the filtration: every element of π_m(S^2) lies in V_3 because maps to S^2 have zero 3-dilation; the nontrivial elements of π_4(S^3) and π_5(S^3) lie in V_3; one nontrivial element of π_6(S^3) lies in V_3; the nontrivial elements of π_5(S^4) and π_6(S^4) are shown to lie in V_4; and one nontrivial element of π_7(S^4) lies in V_3. The paper notes that lower bounds for k-dilation with k > 2 are much rarer, and specifically says it does not know a way to lower-bound the 3-dilation of maps representing torsion homotopy classes; for example, the 100-fold suspension of the Hopf map in π_103(S^102) is known from Proposition 1 to have representatives with arbitrarily small 69-dilation, but the paper leaves open whether it can have arbitrarily small 3-dilation.
why_relevant: This paper gives key constructions showing how small k-dilation can coexist with nontrivial topology in some dimensions. Even though it is mainly constructive, it helps identify which features of the degree-one rectangle problem must be used to rule out rank-collapse/Kaufman-type behavior.
